coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of biomass-derived carbon nanodots: Characterization and applications
coredata.dc:creator: Varsha Raveendran, P.T.
Renuka, N.K.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs or C-dots), the novel entry to the nanocarbon family, have found a distinct place in material science. Herein, the cytotoxicity of fluorescent CDs derived from the leaves of Lour (L-CDs) and Thumbai (T-CDs) are evaluated in vitro towards HeLa cancer cells and normal primary H8 cells. Carbon dots synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method are characterized using HR-TEM, FT-IR, UV–Visible, photoluminescence, and FT-Raman analysis spectroscopies. Both MTT (3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay and cell morphology images depict the dose-dependent reduction in cell viability in malignant cells, while commendably good tolerance was noted in primary H8 cells. The IC50 value obtained for T-CDs is 2 μg/mL for cancer cell lines, which is extremely appealing. Caspase 3 assay and reactive oxygen species analysis were also conducted, which conforms the apoptosis-mediated cell death. Besides the anticancer efficacy, the antioxidant nature of the particles is also traced using these particles through DPPH radical scavenging analysis (RSA). A comparatively less explored feature of carbon dots, reducing nature, is also demonstrated here.

a) FTIR profile of CDs, b) Raman spectrum of L-CDs c) Raman spectrum of T-CDs, d) XRD spectral profile of L-CDs e) XRD pattern of T-CDs.
a) UV Visible spectral profile of CDs, b) Photoluminescence spectra of CDs, c) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs, (d& e) excitation dependent emission spectra of L-CDs and T-CDs respectively.
Dose-dependent in vitro cytotoxicity against HeLa cells by a) L-CDs, b) T-CDs; c) Effect of both CDs on normal primary H8 cells.
Morphology of HeLa cells under (a & d) control, b & e) L-CDs & T-CDs at their IC25 concentration, c & f) each CDs at their IC50 concentration.

The photographic images of intensification of color during the reaction progress is shown in the inset. head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of biomass-derived carbon nanodots: Characterization and applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Materials Research Centre, Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala, 673 635, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Materials Research Centre, Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala, 673 635, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs or C-dots), the novel entry to the nanocarbon family, have found a distinct place in material science. Herein, the cytotoxicity of fluorescent CDs derived from the leaves of Lour (L-CDs) and Thumbai (T-CDs) are evaluated in vitro towards HeLa cancer cells and normal primary H8 cells. Carbon dots synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method are characterized using HR-TEM, FT-IR, UV–Visible, photoluminescence, and FT-Raman analysis spectroscopies. Both MTT (3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay and cell morphology images depict the dose-dependent reduction in cell viability in malignant cells, while commendably good tolerance was noted in primary H8 cells. The IC50 value obtained for T-CDs is 2 μg/mL for cancer cell lines, which is extremely appealing. Caspase 3 assay and reactive oxygen species analysis were also conducted, which conforms the apoptosis-mediated cell death. Besides the anticancer efficacy, the antioxidant nature of the particles is also traced using these particles through DPPH radical scavenging analysis (RSA). A comparatively less explored feature of carbon dots, reducing nature, is also demonstrated here.

ce:section.ce:para: Ever since its discovery in 2004 by Xu et al. [1], the elite member of the nano-carbon fraternity, carbon dots (CDs or C-dots), have riveted the scientific community with their magical properties including good aqueous solubility [2,3], high chemical stability [4], facile modification [5], biocompatibility [6–8], and the unique fluorescence [5]. Due to these distinct properties, CDs have been explored for versatile applications in fields such as sensing [9–11], energy [4,12,13], catalysis [14,15], and biomedicine [16]. Various methods like laser ablation [17,18], chemical oxidation [19], solvothermal treatment [20], electrochemical exfoliation [21], microwave irradiation [22], as well as hydrothermal carbonization of the precursor moieties were adopted to synthesize these zero-dimensional nanoparticles, proceeding via top-down and bottom-up approach [5,23,24].

In the past few decades, “green synthesis” strategies are very much in practice for achieving carbon dots via sustainable and eco-friendly routes. To date, various plant parts like flowers [25], leaves [26], fruits [27], vegetables [28], seeds [29], etc. have been used to yield CDs of multifaceted applications [30]. Among these, plant leaves might be the best choice as a precursor since they contain a rich amount of hydrocarbons in the form of polyphenols, alkaloids, terpenoids, etc [31]. In addition, plant leaf-mediated synthesis procedures are preferred due to their high biodiversity, safest execution, and non-invasiveness to the source plant. The economic aspects are also definitely counted. Leaves of mango [32], spinach [33], bamboo [34], lotus [35], palm [35], mint [36], maple [35], willow [37], etc. have been used for the production of C-dots. Most of these particles are screened for their applications in sensing, patterning, and bioimaging by exploiting their luminescence-based fine features. The medicinal importance of the cocktail of plant leaves are known from ancient time and are well explored in various biological applications including anticancer [38], antioxidant [39], anti-inflammatory [40], and antimicrobial fields [41]. But the usage of toxic solvents, tedious extraction procedures as well as the low stability of the prepared extracts limit the practical application of the plant leaf extracts as such. Thanks to the advantageous of highly stable CDs derived from leaf sources, that possess equal or even better medicinal properties.

Some recent reports have shown that nanoparticle-assisted treatment is a fine alternative in cancer treatment compared to chemotherapy, radiation, and hormone therapy [42]. The application of carbon dots are also been extended to the area of photodynamic therapy, where efficient cancer-killing was made possible by CDs after light treatment [43]. Lately, by making use of their selective toxicity towards cancer cells, the carbon dots have been explored as such in cancer-killing which mainly depends on the nature of the precursor [44]. The cancer cell growth inhibition ability of carbon dots derived from used green tea [45], ginger [46], mushroom [47], walnut oil [48], and dates pits [42] were studied by the researchers. Recently, Lu et al. has reported the excellent anti-tumor activity of gallic acid-derived carbon dots on HeLa cells [49]. The prodrug activating ability of hemoglobin-derived carbon dots was established after assessing their tumor-killing efficiency on HeLa cells, where 90% killing occurred at a 1 mg/mL concentration of CDs [50]. And more recently, Ankireddy et al. has exploited persimmon fruit-derived carbon dots and their nanohybrids in HeLa cell proliferation [51].

The present investigation highlights the ability of carbon dots achieved from Plectranthus Amboinicus (Lour) and Leucas lavandulifolia (Thumbai) leaves in the growth inhibition of HeLa cells. The results indicated the extremely high proliferation ability of CDs towards the above-mentioned cell lines with negligible toxicity to normal H8 cell lines even at higher concentrations. The efficiency of present systems in cancer cell killing prompted us to investigate their free radical scavenging ability also. There are only a few reports on the antioxidant nature of natural source-derived carbon dots except that from coconut shell [52], coriander leaf [26], garlic [53], etc. Since the antioxidant property is directly related to the reducing nature [54], it is noteworthy to check the reducing efficiency of CDs which is attempted via Fe3+ reduction in the present case. Carbon dots-based nano-materials have been exploited for the reduction of metal ions like silver and gold [55]. But the reports that discuss its role in ferric ion reduction are rare. The only one reported precedented work in Fe3+ reduction is by Masoudshariaty et al., who utilized onion and grape CDs for this reduction reaction [56]. Rapid conversion of Fe3+to Fe2+ was achieved through reaction devoid of other chemical reagents and heat treatment in presence of these green reductant CDs.

Lour (Plectranthus amboinicus) leaves were collected from the home garden and Thumbai (Leucas lavandulifoia) leaves were obtained from a field near to home (Olipram Kadavu, Malappuram, Kerala, India). The plants were taxonomically identified by Dr. A.K Pradeep, Assistant Professor, and Taxonomist, Department of Botany, University of Calicut, Kerala, India. Voucher specimens of these two leaves were preserved in Calicut University Herbarium with the accession numbers 7080, and 7081 respectively. Sodium hydroxide pellets, ferric chloride salt, 1,10 phenanthroline, Anhydrous Sodium chloride, and hydrochloric acid were purchased from Merck Ltd, India. Silver nitrate, chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), fetal bovine, DMSO, p-formaldehyde, p-nitroanilide, antibiotic-antimycotic (1%), DPPH, and l-glutamine were obtained from Sigma Aldrich.

In a typical carbon dots synthesis procedure, 5 g of the leaves were washed well with deionized water, chopped finely, and crushed to make a slurry in a mortar. Then dispersed in 40 mL of deionized water and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 200 0c for 5 h in a muffle furnace. After the reaction was completed, the insoluble part was filtered off and the CDs were collected by centrifugation at 3500 rpm for 1 h. The samples were preserved at 4 °C for further studies. The CDs solution obtained from Lour, and Thumbai are named as L-CDs, and T-CDs respectively.

The morphology, particle size, and shape of prepared carbon dots were analyzed through the Jeol/JEM 2100 HR-TEM (High-resolution transmission electron microscopy) technique. Samples for TEM analysis were prepared by dropping an aqueous solution of carbon dots on a carbon-coated copper grid. The idea about the crystallinity/amorphous nature of carbon dots was obtained from XRD (X-ray diffraction) analysis. Rigaku MiniFlex diffractometer with Cu Kα (λ = 0.154 nm) radiation source in the range from 00 to 900 was employed for the XRD analysis of CDs. The information about surface functional groups present in the synthesized CDs was obtained from FTIR (Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy) spectral studies. A JASCO FTIR-4100 instrument was used for FTIR analysis of CDs samples via the KBr disc method. Further insight into the CDs structure is obtained by using Raman spectral analysis. For recording the Raman spectra, a Lab Ram HR-Horiba Jobinyvon spectrometer with Raman microprobe with 532 nm Nd: YAG excitation source was employed. The surface charge of the prepared CDs was measured with the help of Malvern Zetasizer. JASCO V-550 spectrophotometer was used to record the absorbance spectrum of carbon dot solution. 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cuvette was used for analyzing the spectrum. The photoluminescence spectra of CDs were recorded with the help of Agilent Cary Eclipse Spectrophotometer. The fluorescence lifetime of the prepared CDs was monitored using Horiba Fluorolog fluorescence spectrometer with TCSPC at an excitation wavelength of 375 nm. Morphology of the cells was observed using Nikon (Japan) bright-field inverted light microscopy (Nikon Japan). The absorbance spectra for evaluating the cytotoxicity of samples were recorded with a microplate reader (Bio-TekELx800).

Cervical cancer (HeLa) cells for cytotoxic studies were collected from the National Center for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune. The cancer cells were kept in Dulbecco's modified eagles medium (DMEM), which is further supplemented with balanced salt solution (BSS) adjusted to contain 1.5 g/L Na2CO3, 0.1 mM non-essential amino acids, 2 mM l-glutamine, 1 mM sodium pyruvate, 1.5 g/L glucose, 10 mM (4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazine ethane sulfonic acid) (HEPES) and 10% fetal bovine serum. Penicillin and streptomycin (100 IU/100 μg) were adjusted to 1 mL/L. The cells were incubated further at a temperature of 37 °C with 5% CO2 in a humidified CO2 incubator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cytotoxicity by MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay
ce:section.ce:para:
MTT assay was performed to investigate the cytotoxic property of the prepared carbon dots against HeLa cells and primary line H8 cells, which is non-cancerous. Briefly, cancer cells were seeded into a 96-well plate at a density of 5.0 × 103 cells/well in 100 μL of complete Dulbecco's Modified Eagles Medium (DMEM) containing 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and cultured for 1 day at 37 °C in 5% CO2 atmosphere. After that, the MTT stock solution prepared (5 mg/mL in PBS, 20 lL) was added to each well and incubated for 4 h. Then after removing the media, Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well and the absorbance measurements were carried out using Bio-TekELx800 instrument. The percentage of viability of cells was evaluated using the following formula, where OD is the optical density.
% of viability = (OD value of sample/OD value of control) x 100.

1 × 105 HeLa cells/coverslip were grown and incubated with carbon dots at their IC25 and IC50 concentration. Later, they were fixed in a methanol: acetic acid mixture. The morphology of the cells was analyzed after the coverslips were slowly mounted on glass slides. The changes that had to occur to cell morphology were analyzed by using Nikon (Japan) bright-field inverted light microscopy.

The reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated in HeLa cells with incubation with CDs in the present investigation were measured using 5-(and-6)-carboxy-2′ 7′-dichlorodihydro Fluorescein diacetate (carboxy-H2DCFDA). For in vitro studies, 100 μL of HeLa cells (1 × 106 cells/well) in a 96-well cell culture plate was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Later, these cells were further incubated with CDs for another 24 h. After that, these cell samples were washed with PBS (50 μL) three times, then aliquots of carboxy- H2DCFDA (10 μmol L−1) were separately added to the cells which were incubated for another 30 min. A microplate fluorometer was employed for capturing the fluorescence of the oxidized form of carboxy-H2DCFDA.

Briefly, 100 μL of HeLa cells in a 96-well cell culture plate (1 × 103/well) was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Following incubation, these cells were further incubated with IC25 and IC50 concentrations of CDs for 24 h. After the cells were washed with PBS three times, Then, 100 μL per well of Caspase-3 substrate were separately added to the cells. The caspase activity was estimated by measuring the absorbance of p-nitroanilide (pNA) at the wavelength of 405 nm.

The ability of the prepared CDs in scavenging DPPH radicals was investigated by the reported method. The addition of 0.1 mM DPPH solution in various concentrations (10,20, 30, 40, and 50 μg/mL) of CDs in the presence of Tris–HCl buffer (50 mM, pH 7.4) resulted in the reduction in absorbance. The DPPH scavenging efficiency was evaluated by using the following formula.
Inhibition % = (Ac–As)/Ac
Where Ac and As respectively indicate the absorbance of DPPH in the absence and presence of the radical scavenger, in this case, the carbon dots.

Fe3+→Fe2+reduction reaction
The ferric to ferrous ions reduction was easily achieved by using the caron dots solution of our interest. The yellow-colored ferric chloride (1 mM) solution was turned to pale green immediately after the addition of a very small quantity of carbon dots (100 μL). The absorbance of the solution was recorded by using a UV–Visible spectrometer. The reduction process was further confirmed by using a ferrous ion complexing agent, 1, 10 phenanthroline (1, 10 phen). In a typical procedure, 1.5 mL of ferric chloride solution (1 mM) and 100 μL of 1,10 phenanthroline (10 mM) were mixed well in a cuvette. Then, 200 μL of CDs solution was added to the above mixture and the color change was monitored. The absorbance curves of the solution were scanned using a UV Visible spectrophotometer.

The successful formation of luminescent C-dots was achieved through the hydrothermal carbonization of Lour (Plectranthus Amboinicus) leaves and Thumbai (Leucas lavandulifolia) leaves, which are referred to as L-CDs and T-CDs respectively. The color of L-CDs and T-CDs solutions was pale yellow and brown respectively under visible light. HRTEM images presented in Fig. 1 a and c confirm the spherical morphology of both CDs system, without apparent aggregation regardless of the nature of precursors [35]. The inset of Fig. 1a and c shows the lattice fringes at higher magnification, which is consistent with their graphitic nature. Fig. 1b and d provide the size distribution histograms of the synthesized systems, where the average size for L-CDs and T-CDs was found to be 2.9 nm & 3.8 nm respectively. The selected area diffraction pattern (SAED) of each CDs is presented in the insets. Rather non-uniform distribution in particle size was noted in carbon dots derived from Lour leaves.

The presence of various functional groups in carbon dots of our interest was confirmed by FTIR spectral analysis. In the FTIR profile (Fig. 2 a) of L-CDs, the O–H and –NH stretching were observed at 3417 cm−1 and 3264 cm−1 respectively [26,57]. The peaks at 2931 cm−1 and 2854 cm−1 were assigned to –CH stretching vibrations [58]. The stretching vibrations of CO groups were identified at 1635 cm−1 [59].Furthermore, the peaks around 1449 cm−1 and 1394 cm−1 were assigned to CC stretching vibrations [59]. The bands detected at1317 cm−1 and 1112 cm−1 were attributed to the C–N and C–O stretches [35]. The presence of ‘N’ is justified by various components present in the leaf extract. The FTIR spectrum of T-CDs shows O–H and –NH stretching vibrations around 3407 cm−1 and 3233 cm−1 respectively [57,60]. The FTIR bands at 2930 cm−1 and 2844 cm−1 correspond to the –CH stretching vibrations of CH3 and CH2 groups respectively, whereas CC stretching vibrations of the aromatic ring have appeared around 1449 cm−1 and 1394 cm−1. The Carbonyl stretching vibrations of carboxylic groups present in T-CDs were observed at 1643 cm−1. Notably, the peak at 1309 cm−1 corresponded to C–N stretching vibrations, while the bands around 1112 cm−1and 1048 cm−1 represent stretching and bending vibrations of the C–O bond of carboxylic groups [57]. The presence of hydroxyl, carboxylic, carbonyl and amino functional groups endow CDs with good water solubility. No loss of fluorescence was noticed for both the solutions even after a long period indicating high photo-stability of the systems. Further information regarding the structure of CDs was achieved using Raman spectroscopy. Fig. 2b and c provide the Raman spectra of L-CDs and T-CDs respectively. L-CDs show D band at 1297 cm−1 and G band at 1597 cm−1 whereas the corresponding bands were noted at 1392 cm−1 and 1549 cm−1 for T-CDs. The observed D band originating from sp3 carbon and G band arising from sp2 carbon are associated with the A1g symmetry photons of the K zone boundary and E 2g, the sp2 carbon vibrational mode. The relative intensities (ID/IG) of L-CDs and T-CDs were 1.27 and 1.09 respectively, which signifies the as-prepared carbon dots have more structural defects in them. This justifies a functionalized carbon core, as hinted by the FTIR spectral data. The presence of vacant lattice sites in the sp3 carbon is also indicated [44]. Information on the graphitic nature of carbon core is gathered from X-ray diffraction studies. The XRD peak of L-CDs was centered around 23.940 and that of T-CDs is at 24.340 (Fig. 2d and e). This indicates (002) lattice spacing with a high level of topological disorder in carbon nanoparticles [61]. The d spacing values obtained for L-CDs and T-CDs are 0.371 nm and 0.365 nm respectively, which are slightly greater than that of the normal graphitic distance (0.34 nm), which indicate a disordered graphitic core for the carbon particles, with increased amorphous nature and abundant sp3 disorder in them [62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of leaf derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The optical features of both the leaf-derived CDs were probed thoroughly. L-CDs and T-CDs exhibit pale yellowish and dark brown colors respectively under daylight. Fig. 3 a displays the UV–visible absorption spectrum of the as-synthesized CDs, where L-CDs and T-CDs show almost similar absorption bands, around 273 nm, and 279 nm respectively. A shoulder peak around 320 nm was assigned to the n = π∗ transitions of CO functional groups present on the surface of CDs [63]. This in turn indicates a functionalized carbon core in the particles. The inherent feature of carbon dots that makes them distinct in the nanocarbon family is their luminescence. Fig. 3b provides the photoluminescence spectral profiles of CDs. At an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, maximum emission occurred at 438 nm and 446 nm for L-CDs and T-CDs respectively. The inset of Fig. 3b shows the photographic images of the solutions under daylight and UV light. The systems were luminescent in bright blue and greenish-blue respectively, when illuminated under UV light of wavelength 365 nm. Time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) analysis was carried out to measure the average fluorescence lifetime of the synthesized carbon dots samples at 440 nm at an excitation wavelength of 375 nm. The log plots of PL decay of the two systems are given in Fig. 3c, where the calculated average lifetime, τav of L-CDs and T-CDs were noted to be 5.11ns (Chsq = 1.2120) and 4.94 ns (Chsq = 1.1425) respectively. A relatively short lifetime decay value indicates that the fluorescence emission is the result of radiative recombination of excitons [64]. The obtained lifetime values in the nanosecond range signify the potential of the synthesized carbon dots in biological and optoelectronic fields.

Like most fluorescent carbon dots, the present systems also exhibit an important emission behavior which is nothing but the excitation-dependent photoluminescence characteristics. The PL of CDs was recorded at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 360 to 420 nm [53,65]. In the case of L-CDs, as the excitation wavelength changes from 360 to 420 nm, the emission is shifted from 438 to 488 nm (Fig. 3d). In the case of T-CDs, the emission wavelength was red-shifted to 502 nm when excited at 420 nm (Fig. 3e). The presence of differently sized particles, different surface emissive traps, and surface detects in carbon dots constitute the reasons for this kind of excitation-dependent emission feature. Zeta potential measurement is a technique for the direct measurement of the surface charge of nanoparticles. Nanoparticles having both high negative and positive zeta potential values indicate a high level of colloidal stability. The zeta potential values are found to be – 4 mV and – 4.42 mV respectively for L-CDs and T-CDs. Such negative zeta potential values indicate a negatively charged surface of CDs with abundant hydroxyl and carboxylic functional groups and good aqueous stability of the systems.

To evaluate the stability of as-synthesized L-CDs and T-CDs systems, the fluorescence intensity was monitored under continuous UV irradiation and in different ionic concentrations. 210 min of continuous UV irradiation has no obvious influence on the PL intensity of either of them (Fig. 1a and b of ESI). Besides, both the CDs are stable in a wide range of NaCl concentrations from 1 mM to 100 mM (Fig. 1c and d of ESI). The above results revealed that L-CDs and T-CDs are optically stable and can be applied in biological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of CDs towards HeLa cells

ce:section.ce:para: MTT assay was carried out to examine the cytotoxicity of CDs on human cervical cancer cell line HeLa and normal H8 cells. Fig. 4 a and b shows the dose-dependent in-vitro cytotoxicity of L-CDs and T-CDs on HeLa cells in 48 h time duration. With increasing the concentration of L-CDs from 0 to 18 μg/mL, about 90% of the cells were found to be dead. The 50% inhibiting concentration (IC50) value of the L-CDs on HeLa cells was 10 μg/mL. Compared to L-CDs, T-CDs exhibited an increased inhibition effect on HeLa cells, where 90% of cells were inhibited at a concentration of 4 μg/mL and the IC50 value was found to be 2 μg/mL. From the MTT study, it is clear that both CDs were able to reduce the viability of HeLa cells, where the growth inhibition is concentration-dependent and can be effectively applied at a lower dosage. The small difference in the cytotoxic activity of two CDs may be associated with surface hydroxyl groups on CDs which is believed to be one of the factors for reactive oxygen species generation and thereby cell death process. On the other hand, no significant cytotoxicity effect of CDs (0–200 μg/mL) on normal H8 cells was observed after 48 h of exposure (Fig. 4c) ensuring the safe usage of the mentioned systems as anticancer agents. Only less than 10% of the normal cells were inhibited at this concentration, recommending successful application in treating cancer. The high specificity of CDs towards cancerous cells over other normal cells can be related to the overexpression of H2O2 in cancerous cells compared to the latter, which will lead to the generation of reactive oxygen species and thereby cause cell death in the former [50]. Among the two systems, T-CDs are suggested to be more suitable for cancer treatment.

The morphology of the HeLa cells in the presence and absence of carbon dots was monitored by using a Nikon light inverted microscope. The photographic image of cells incubated with two different concentrations of carbon dots (IC25 & IC50) are provided in Fig. 5 . The control cells (untreated) appeared normal, whereas the CDs treated cells showed distinct cellular morphology. The treated cells appeared damaged and are different from that of normal cells. Among these, cells treated with IC50 concentration of CDs appeared to be more affected by the action as seen from the images, due to the higher dosage administered. Irregular cell membranes observed in the images serve as apparent evidence for apoptotic activation [66].

Since the excess levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in cells result in apoptotic mediated cell killing [45,67,68], it is important to confirm the generation of ROS in the HeLa cells treated with CDs system. To this effect, one of our systems, T-CDs which is found to be more efficient in cancer killing is subjected for ROS analysis. The formation of ROS in the HeLa cells as a result of T-CDs incubation was assessed by using 5-(and-6)-carboxy-2′7′-dichlorodihydro Fluorescein diacetate (H2DCFDA) [69]. The diffusion of H2DCFDA to cells and the deacetylation by cellular esterases lead to the formation of 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein (H2DCF) [70]. In the presence of ROS, predominantly H2O2, H2DCF is rapidly oxidized to 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF) [68,71], which is highly fluorescent, with excitation and emission wavelengths of 498 and 522 nm, respectively. Fig. 6 a displays the fluorescence of the oxidized form of carboxy-H2DCFDA treated with T-CDs at their IC25 and IC50 concentrations within 1 h at an excitation wavelength of 498 nm (emission wavelength of 522 nm). Compared to the control, there occurred an enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of CDs treated cells, indicating more number of reactive oxygen species produced through apoptosis in the latter, which causes oxidation of non-fluorescent H2DCF to fluorescent DCF [71]. Fig. 6b further confirms this result.

Activation of caspase is important for the initiation and evolution of programmed cell death (apoptosis) in various biological systems [72,73]. Caspase-3 [74], a member of this family has an important role in apoptotic cell death, mediated through mitochondria [48]. Active caspase-3 cleaves and activates other caspases and relevant targets in the cytoplasm [74]. The activity of caspase-3 in HeLa cells was investigated for analyzing the type of cell death involved. The dose-dependent increase of caspase-3 is represented in Fig. 2 of ESI. 24 h treatment with both IC25 and IC50 concentration of T-CDs increased caspase-3 activation in human carcinoma cell lines, as evident from the figure. The dose-dependent caspase-3 activation revealed that the cell death proceeds through caspase mediated apoptotic pathway.

Encouraged by the efficient anticancer activity that the CDs exhibited, we have further taken an attempt to check their antioxidant capacity. For this, the radical scavenging activity of the two CDs was evaluated by the well-known DPPH method. The DPPH radical scavenging activity of the two CDs is determined by recording the absorbance at 517 nm wavelengths. The dose-dependent scavenging of DPPH radical by L-CDs and T-CDs at concentrations from 0 to 50 μg/mL is illustrated in Fig. 7 a and b respectively. The absorbance gradually decreased upon increasing the concentration of CDs, where L-CDs and T-CDs shows efficient radical scavenging activity with 84 ± 0.12 and 89 ± 0.17% respectively at their 50 μg/mL concentration. Based on the % inhibition obtained (Fig. 7 c), the IC50 calculated for L-CDs is 19.04 μg/mL and for T-CDs 17.25 μg/mL. The order is in line with the anticancer efficiency of the two systems.

Recently, two research groups have demonstrated the radical scavenging activity of garlic and date molasses derived carbon dots, where the IC50 value was obtained to be 80 μg/mL and 40 μg/mL respectively by the DPPH method [53,75]. Another team studied the antioxidant effect of carbon dots derived from PEI and citric acid, where the IC50 value was reported to be 197 μg/mL [76]. In comparison, both of our systems namely L-CDs and T-CDs in this work showed good radical scavenging activity with lower IC50 values. More recently, Jiao et al. have reported the usage of color-tunable carbon dots from p-phenylenediamine for antioxidant applications, which exhibited above 94% scavenging activity at 7.5 μg/mL CDs concentration [77]. Even though the as mentioned system is more efficient in radical scavenging activity compared to our system, it utilized toxic chemicals as the precursor for achieving CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduction of Fe3+ using leaf derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: After being satisfied with the efficient antioxidant potential of the present systems, we have attempted to study the reducing nature of the as-synthesized CDs. To date, the reducing ability of CDs has been mainly used in Ag or Au nanoparticles synthesis. Mainly, the hydroxylic functional groups present on the surface of CDs bestow reducing property to carbon dots [56]. The reports regarding carbon-based nanomaterials as a reductant for Fe3+ ions are rare. Herein, we have demonstrated the reducing nature of the present systems on ferric ions by converting Fe (III) to Fe (II) in a rapid manner. Upon the addition of both L-CDs and T-CDs to ferric chloride solution (1 mM), the color of the iron solution has changed to pale green ensuring the formation of Fe2+. The appearance of a broad peak around 500–700 nm region of the UV visible spectra of the mixture (ferric chloride and CDs) confirms the mentioned change (Fig. 8 a). The ferric to ferrous conversion was further proven by using a ferrous ion complexing agent namely, 1,10 phenanthroline (1,10 phen). Fig. 8b displays the spectra of a mixture of Fe3+ (1.5 mL, 1 mM) and 1, 10 phen (100 μL, 9 mM) in the presence and absence of CDs. As evident from the spectra, a new peak around 500 nm was observed in the presence of both L-CDs and T-CDs with a slight difference in the absorbance. The above-mentioned peak has originated from the complexation of Fe2+ which is accompanied by the color change to orange (inset figure).

To analyze the role of time in the reduction process, absorption spectra of the mixture of CDs (200 μL), ferric chloride (1.5 mL, 1 mM) and 1,10 phen (100 μL, 9 mM) at different time intervals were recorded. Fig. 8c and d show the UV Visible profiles of the mixture of Fe (III), phen and L-CDs and T-CDs respectively. The absorbance around 500 nm is increased with time for both L-CDs and T-CDs and stable values were achieved in 30 min and 20 min respectively. After that, no observable variation occurs which signifies the completion of reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ within the mentioned time intervals. The inset of both the figures is the photographs of the mixtures which further confirms the time-dependent reduction process in the two cases. As discussed earlier, it is also evident from the spectral data that the reduction process is slightly faster with T-CDs compared to L-CDs.

In summary, we have proposed an easy, green, and economic route for fabricating fluorescent CDs with the use of two medicinally important plant leaves of lamiaceae family as the precursor via a one-pot green hydrothermal method. The CDs are spherical particles having a size under 5 nm and exhibited bluish luminescence. Both CDs exhibit interesting differential cytotoxicity against HeLa cells with an evident concentration-dependent reduction in cell viability after 48 h of exposure with negligible killing effect on non-cancerous primary H8 human cells. The IC50 values were found to be 10 μg/mL and 2 μg/mL for L-CDs and T-CDs respectively. Along with antitumor activity, CDs demonstrated excellent radical scavenging activity on DPPH and reducing action on ferric ions. In brief, the extremely high anticancer activity of the CDs with excellent tolerability in non-cancerous primary H8 cells along with antioxidant efficacy suggests a promising future potential as efficient theranostic agents.
P.T. Varsha Raveendran: Investigation, Writing – original draft. N.K. Renuka: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – original draft.
